channel.title: NYT > U.S. News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Raman Gains on Pratt in L.A. Mayor Race But Many Ballots Remain
item.description: Nithya Raman’s vote count still remains well behind Spencer Pratt’s, but hundreds of thousands of ballots remain to be counted in Los Angeles over the next few weeks.
item.dc:creator: William P. Davis

item.title: Platner Denies Hurting Ex-Girlfriend and Says He Will Not Quit Senate Race
item.description: In an interview after The Times reported on his treatment of women he had dated, Graham Platner acknowledged “not exactly acting with the best behavior” after his military service.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Joe Raedle/Getty Images
item.media:description: Graham Platner, the Democratic candidate for Senate in Maine, said in an interview he would “absolutely take responsibility” for elements of his personal history.

item.title: Court Reverses Convictions of Paramedics in Elijah McClain’s Death
item.description: A Colorado appeals court ordered new trials for Peter Cichuniec and Jeremy Cooper, convicted in 2023 after injecting Mr. McClain with ketamine. The court cited errors in jury instructions.
item.dc:creator: Mark Walker
item.media:credit: David Zalubowski/Associated Press
item.media:description: A Colorado court reversed homicide convictions against two paramedics in the death of Elijah McClain, who died in 2019 after he was given a ketamine injection.

item.title: Murder Charge Dropped for Arkansas Man Nominated for Lonoke County Sheriff
item.description: A judge dismissed the charge against Aaron Spencer, the Republican nominee for Lonoke County sheriff, citing missing evidence and misconduct by law enforcement officials.
item.dc:creator: Pooja Salhotra
item.media:credit: Heather Spencer, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Aaron Spencer had been accused of killing his daughter’s abuser before the dismissal.

item.title: What to Know About California’s Top Election Races
item.description: The Republicans Steve Hilton, who is running for governor, and Spencer Pratt, running for Los Angeles mayor, have shown early strength, but that could fade as more ballots are counted.
item.dc:creator: Jill Cowan and Livia Albeck-Ripka
item.media:credit: Ariana Drehsler for The New York Times
item.media:description: Voters in Los Angeles on Tuesday.

item.title: House Passes Ukraine Aid in Defiance of Republican Leaders
item.description: Eighteen G.O.P. lawmakers broke with their party and joined Democrats to deliver yet another blow to the president’s foreign policy agenda.
item.dc:creator: Robert Jimison
item.media:credit: Tyler Hicks/The New York Times
item.media:description: Ukrainian soldiers in the Donetsk region of eastern Ukraine in January. The House bill passed Thursday included $8 billion in loans to Ukraine and $1.8 billion in aid.

item.title: Ohio State Agrees to $100 Million Payout Over Sexual Abuse Claims
item.description: The deal with 279 former students is the latest in the long-running case at the university. In all, nearly 600 people who said they were abused by an athletic department doctor have settled.
item.dc:creator: Billy Witz
item.media:credit: Angie Wang/Associated Press
item.media:description: A sexual abuse scandal has enveloped Ohio State University since 2018, when a former student came forward with claims that he was assaulted by an athletic team doctor.

item.title: California Businessman Accused of Selling Restricted Technology to Iran
item.description: Jamshid Ghomi, of Newport Coast, Calif., was charged with conspiracy to violate the International Emergency Economic Powers Act.
item.dc:creator: Mark Walker
item.category: Office of Foreign Assets Control (United States Treasury)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: A woman walking near a billboard in Tehran last month. Federal prosecutors charged the chief executive of an Iranian-based technology company with conspiring to evade U.S. restrictions on technology.

item.title: Trump’s Fraud Claims in California Could Undermine Confidence in November Result
item.description: The president is claiming without evidence that the lengthy counting process in California, which could help determine control of Congress, means Democrats are stealing the election.
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti
item.media:credit: Jae C. Hong/Associated Press
item.media:description: Election workers processing ballots at the Los Angeles County Ballot Processing Center on Wednesday.

item.title: Trump Plans to Create a Promenade at the Lincoln Memorial
item.description: The president announced a new project that would connect the memorial to the Potomac River. He isn’t sure if he wants it to be named after himself.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater
item.media:credit: Allison Robbert for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump announced his plans to create a promenade in the back of the Lincoln Memorial, which sits near the Potomac River.

item.title: Postal Service Frees Up Enough Cash to Keep Operating for ‘Several Years’
item.description: The agency had warned it could run out of money within months, but its regulators said suspending payments to a retirement fund had given officials more time to find a permanent solution.
item.dc:creator: Adam Sella
item.media:credit: Scott McIntyre for The New York Times
item.media:description: The U.S. Postal Service has raised prices and slowed delivery standards in recent years as it struggles to stanch declining mail volume and rising structural costs.

item.title: Vanilla Ice Is In, Bret Michaels Is Out: Trump’s Battle for Celebrity Validation
item.description: President Trump has pursued fame his entire adult life. Now in his second term in the White House, he is finding how little power he has to force cultural figures to fall in line.
item.dc:creator: Shawn McCreesh
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Donald Trump used the Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts to host a red carpet premiere of the “Melania” movie in January. Mr. Trump’s recent posts were a reminder that the onetime reality television star turned president remains fixated and often tortured by celebrity.

item.title: ICE Agent Charged in Minnesota Shooting Will Not Waive Extradition
item.description: Christian Castro, who is jailed in Texas, was charged with assault in connection with a January shooting that wounded a man.
item.dc:creator: Mitch Smith
item.media:credit: David Guttenfelder/The New York Times
item.media:description: Federal agents patrolled Minneapolis after the shooting on Jan. 14.

item.title: Several Women Who Dated Graham Platner Recall ‘Unsettling’ Behavior
item.description: The Democratic candidate for Senate in Maine could be charming, women said in interviews, but some found his actions intimidating and disturbing.
item.dc:creator: Katie Glueck and Lisa Lerer
item.media:credit: Gabe Souza for The New York Times
item.media:description: Graham Platner, a combat veteran, has spoken openly about his past struggles with post-traumatic stress disorder, depression and drinking, and has said his behavior years ago does not reflect who he is today.

item.title: Diane Carlson Evans Dies at 79; Led Campaign for Vietnam Women’s Memorial
item.description: A former Army nurse who served in combat, she coordinated a 10-year effort to create a monument recognizing the contributions of the war’s thousands of nurses.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/Associated Press
item.media:description: Ms. Carlson Evans in 1993, after the unveiling of the Vietnam Women’s Memorial in Washington.

item.title: Priest Who Said Aliens Were Demons Removed as Exorcist for Washington
item.description: Cardinal Robert McElroy, archbishop of Washington, said recent statements by the exorcist “undermine the Church’s very precise teaching on the devil.”
item.dc:creator: Ruth Graham
item.media:credit: Andrew Medichini/Associated Press
item.media:description: Monsignor Stephen Rossetti is an ordained priest and psychologist who also served as an exorcist for the archdiocese, a role that involves investigating claims of demonic possession.

item.title: Senators Cassidy and Booker Ask Judge to Maintain Block on $1.8 Billion Fund
item.description: Bill Cassidy, a Republican, and Cory Booker, a Democrat, maintained that the fund was “an immediate and dire threat to our constitutional order and the authority of Congress.”
item.dc:creator: Alan Feuer

item.title: Jan. 6 Rioter Is Hired to Work in Sensitive Pentagon Office
item.description: It was not clear who hired Elias Irizarry, who pleaded guilty to various offenses at the Capitol and was later pardoned by President Trump.
item.dc:creator: Helene Cooper and Alan Feuer
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
Citadel, The Military College of South Carolina
item.media:credit: Justice Department
item.media:description: A Justice Department photo shows Elias Irizarry in a conference room at the Capitol on Jan. 6, 2021, when he was videotaped by one of his friends sitting in an armchair with a pole across his lap.

item.title: University of Texas to Remove Cesar Chavez Statue From Austin Campus
item.description: The university joins a list of schools and cities across the U.S. that have removed memorials and images featuring the labor leader after abuse allegations surfaced.
item.dc:creator: Lauren McGaughy
item.media:credit: Mikala Compton/Austin American-Statesman, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Campus workers in Austin begin to remove the Cesar Chavez statue from the West Lawn in Austin, Texas, Thursday.

item.title: Supreme Court Finds S.E.C. Can Strip Wrongdoers of Illegal Financial Gains, Even Without Proof of Victim Loss
item.description: The justices unanimously sided with the independent financial watchdog, finding that the agency could collect ill-gotten money, even if it could not show victims had suffered financial loss.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle
item.media:credit: Alyssa Schukar for The New York Times
item.media:description: In a unanimous decision written by Justice Neil M. Gorsuch, the justices sided with the Securities and Exchange Commission.

item.title: Granted Clemency by Trump, Scores of Jan. 6 Rioters Have Been Accused of New Crimes
item.description: At least 97 of those who were charged in connection with the Capitol riot have reoffended in the years since the attack, the nonprofit publication Lawfare has found.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump has made clear that he believes the Jan. 6 rioters should not only be praised, but that they should be compensated with taxpayer money.

item.title: Justice Department Tells Prosecutors to Pursue Immigrant Vote Fraud Cases
item.description: About 90 such investigations are currently open, a Justice Department official told prosecutors. The tally offers a glimpse inside the scale of the administration’s efforts.
item.dc:creator: Ernesto Londoño
item.media:credit: Michael A. McCoy for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Department of Justice in Washington.

item.title: Republicans Defeat Move to Bar Trump’s Payout Fund
item.description: The vote on Democrats’ proposal, which would have sent the G.O.P. immigration bill back to committee, kicked off a marathon of votes that stretched all day and overnight.
item.dc:creator: Annie Karni and Robert Jimison
item.media:credit: Demetrius Freeman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Chuck Schumer, Democrat and the minority leader on Capitol Hill in Washington on Tuesday.

item.title: Supreme Court Backs F.C.C. Power to Levy Fines Against Cellphone Carriers
item.description: AT&T and Verizon said they were deprived of their right to a jury trial when the agency penalized the companies for failing to protect consumer information.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross for The New York Times
item.media:description: Two major cellphone carriers, Verizon and AT&T, were fined millions of dollars by the Federal Communications Commission as punishment for what the F.C.C. said was their failure to protect customer data.

item.title: John Bolton, Ex-Trump Adviser, Reaches Deal to Plead Guilty Over Classified Information
item.description: The former Trump national security adviser plans to plead guilty to a count of illegal retention of classified information, which could result in a fine and possibly prison time of up to five years.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett and Michael S. Schmidt
item.category: The Room Where It Happened: A White House Memoir (Book)
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: John R. Bolton’s indictment accused him of mishandling classified information when he compiled notes for a book that was critical of President Trump.

item.title: Rebecca Grossman and Scott Erickson, Ex-MLB Pitcher, Found Liable for $176 Million in Hit-and-Run
item.description: The jury ruled against Rebecca Grossman, a philanthropist, and Scott Erickson, a former M.L.B. pitcher. Ms. Grossman was earlier convicted of murder in the case.
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses
item.media:credit: Irfan Khan/Los Angeles Times, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Rebecca Grossman outside court in Los Angeles in 2024.

item.title: Planning Commission Votes to Advance Trump’s Arch Project
item.description: The National Capital Planning Commission received nearly 1,700 comments about President Trump’s plans to build a 250-foot arch in the nation’s capital. Almost all opposed the idea.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump with a model of his proposed arch at the White House last year.

item.title: Trump Says He Plans to Nominate Blanche for Attorney General
item.description: Todd Blanche has been serving as the acting attorney general. A formal nomination to the post would be the president’s latest move to place loyalists in top government jobs.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Thrush
item.media:credit: Demetrius Freeman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Acting U.S. Attorney General Todd Blanche during a House Committee on Appropriations, Subcommittee on Commerce, Justice, Science, and Related Agencies hearing this week.

item.title: The Democrats’ Big Decision: Black Representation or More Blue Seats?
item.description: As Republicans break up majority-Black House districts, Democrats must decide whether to preserve seats concentrated in urban areas or push them into white suburbs to target G.O.P. seats.
item.dc:creator: Clyde McGrady
item.media:credit: Will Crooks for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative James Clyburn addressing the crowd at his annual fish fry alongside Representative Ro Khanna of California, second from left, and Governor Andy Beshear of Kentucky in Columbia, S.C., on Friday.

item.title: ‘Teachers Are Going to Hate It’: How Social Media Apps Hooked Teens at School
item.description: Internal documents show how tech giants grabbed children’s attention throughout the day, a strategy that schools say has undermined education.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Valentino-DeVries
item.media:credit: Yana Paskova for The New York Times
item.media:description: Social media companies’ push to reach students has often overshadowed concerns from parents and teachers.
